definition: Stock-bond correlation is bifurcated by maturity: short duration (2Y) maintains negative correlation (-0.25 to -0.35) providing hedge value, while long duration (30Y) shows positive correlation (+0.05 to +0.15). Hedging properties depend critically on duration selection, making this operationally essential for portfolio construction.
evidence: Partial fiscal dominance interpretation via Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory
Pattern survived three iterations of cross-agent scrutiny
Falsifiable prediction: 2Y-SPX turning positive signals full fiscal dominance
2Y-SPX correlation at -0.25 to -0.35 directly observable in market data
Agreed in pair_0 debate without challenge
pair_0 debate called this generalist_01's 'most distinctive contribution'
Front end approximates expected policy rate (cyclically responsive); back end reflects fiscal supply (structurally insensitive to business cycle)
KB reports 6 iterations of convergent research from 8 agents
Prescription: bifurcate curve exposure, 2-5Y for equity hedge, 10-30Y as separate correlated risk factor
open_questions: Is the bifurcation over-fitted to a 3-5 year sample with only 4 historical precedents?
Is +0.05 to +0.15 at the long end statistically distinguishable from zero?
Would the 2Y-SPX turning positive reliably signal full fiscal dominance?
Will the bifurcation widen further with fiscal deterioration?
At what point does the front-end hedge also break down?
How stable is the bifurcation across regime transitions?
Could policy intervention cause the bifurcation to collapse within the 3-14 month precedent window?
Where exactly does the transitional zone (50-100bp term premium) lie on the maturity spectrum?
